perturbation.original_text: Within thirty (30) days after the date hereof, GALEN will pay to LEO US$5,000,000 (five million United States dollars) to reimburse LEO for a portion of the actual development costs that have been incurred by LEO. This payment is non-refundable.
perturbation.changed_text: Within ninety (90) days after the date hereof, GALEN will pay to LEO US$5,000,000 (five million United States dollars) to reimburse LEO for a portion of the actual development costs that have been incurred by LEO. This payment is non-refundable. However, LEO must provide an invoice within 14 days of the agreement date; failure to do so invalidates this payment obligation.
perturbation.explanation: Imposing a 14-day invoice deadline for a $5 million payment and invalidating the payment obligation if missed could be seen as unconscionable or a violation of good faith and fair dealing, particularly if there's no reasonable justification for such a short timeframe and high penalty. It could violate general contract law principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law 5-327 addresses unfair and deceptive business practices, and imposing unreasonable deadlines that could result in significant financial penalties could be viewed as such a practice. While not directly applicable, it indicates the spirit of fair dealing required in contracts. The new 14-day condition precedent, and the penalty of invalidating the whole obligation for failure to meet it, could be challenged under general contract principles of fairness and good faith. The original 30-day period is a legally safer, and commercially more reasonable, position.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-327 Consumers' right to recover attorney's fees in actions arising out of consumer contracts General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-327. Consumers' right to recover attorney's fees in actions arising out of consumer contracts. 1. As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) "Consumer contract" means a written agreement entered into between a creditor, seller or lessor as one party with a natural person who is the debtor, buyer or lessee as the second party, and the money, other personal property or services which are the subject of the transaction are primarily for personal, family or household purposes; (b) "Creditor" means a person who regularly extends, or arranges for the extension of, credit which is payable by agreement in more than four installments or for which the payment of a finance charge is or may be required; (c) "Seller" means a person who sells or provides or agrees to sell or provide the subject of a consumer transaction. (d) "Lessor" means a person who regularly leases, or arranges for the lease of, personal property which is the subject of a consumer contract. 2. Whenever a consumer contract provides that the creditor, seller or lessor may recover attorney's fees and expenses incurred as the result of a breach of any contractual obligation by the debtor, buyer or lessee, it shall be implied that the creditor, seller or lessor shall pay the attorney's fees and expenses of the debtor, buyer or lessee incurred as the result of a breach of any contractual obligation by the creditor, seller or lessor, or in the successful defense of any action arising out of the contract commenced by the creditor, seller or lessor. Any limitations on attorney's fees recoverable by the creditor, seller or lessor shall also be applicable to attorney's fees recoverable by the debtor, buyer or lessee under this section. Any waiver of this section shall be void as against public policy.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a 14-day invoice deadline, invalidating the $5 million payment obligation if missed, which raises concerns about fairness and good faith, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (NYS General Obligations Law 5-327) primarily addresses consumer contracts and attorney's fees, it also speaks to the broader principle of fair dealing. Although not directly applicable, it implies that unreasonable deadlines imposing significant financial penalties could be viewed as unfair. The original 30-day period is more reasonable. The new condition precedent and penalty could likely fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: This Article V shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) years, provided, however, that following the termination of this Agreement LEO shall be free to use all data, information or other confidential information relating to the Combination Product and following termination of this Agreement, GALEN shall be free to use all GALEN Information.
perturbation.changed_text: This Article V shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of one (1) year, provided, however, that following the termination of this Agreement LEO shall be free to use all data, information or other confidential information relating to the Combination Product and following termination of this Agreement, GALEN shall be free to use all GALEN Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act, especially for pharmaceutical development, which often has long-term implications and patent considerations. This change could unduly prejudice the disclosing party, undermining trade secret protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to prevent actual or threatened misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. § 1836) provides federal protection for trade secrets. While the Act doesn't dictate a specific confidentiality period, reducing the contractual period to one year for sensitive pharmaceutical information could be deemed inadequate to protect trade secrets, particularly in light of the long development timelines and competitive landscape in the pharmaceutical industry. A one-year period may not be commercially reasonable or sufficient to prevent misappropriation, potentially weakening a party's ability to seek legal recourse under the DTSA if their trade secrets are misappropriated after that period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from ten years to one year. The Law Explanation states that reducing the contractual period to one year for sensitive pharmaceutical information could be deemed inadequate to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 U.S. Code § 1836, allows an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated to bring a civil action. The change weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially undermining a party's ability to seek legal recourse under the Defend Trade Secrets Act if their trade secrets are misappropriated after the one-year period.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that one of the Parties hereto materially defaults or breaches any of the provisions of this Agreement, the other Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon sixty (60) days' written notice, provided, however, that if the Party in default, within the sixty day period referred to, remedies the said default or breach, the Agreement shall continue in full force and effect.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that one of the Parties hereto materially defaults or breaches any of the provisions of this Agreement, the other Party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon ten (10) days' written notice, provided, however, that if the Party in default, within the ten day period referred to, remedies the said default or breach, the Agreement shall continue in full force and effect.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to 10 days for a material breach may be deemed unreasonably short, especially for complex breaches, and may be deemed unconscionable or violate good faith and fair dealing principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although this agreement is not explicitly governed by the UCC, UCC 2-302, as adopted in New York, allows courts to refuse to enforce contracts or clauses deemed unconscionable. Reducing the cure period to ten days for a material breach of a complex development agreement could be viewed as unconscionable, especially if it deprives the breaching party of a reasonable opportunity to cure the breach and avoid termination. The reasonableness of the cure period is a key factor in determining whether a termination clause is enforceable. The original sixty days provided a more commercially reasonable amount of time.
perturbation.location: VII - TERM AND TERMINATION - CONSEQUENCES OF TERMINATION 7.3

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-302 Unconscionable Contract or Clause Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--302. Unconscionable Contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination.

Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period from 60 days to 10 days for a material breach. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this shortened period could be deemed unconscionable, particularly for complex breaches. UCC 2-302, as presented in both scraped snippets, allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause found unconscionable. Reducing the cure period to 10 days could deprive the breaching party of a reasonable opportunity to remedy the breach, leading to a determination of unconscionability, and thus a legal contradiction.
